questions.question: Explain the difference between guided and unguided transmission media, providing one example of each.
keyPoints: Guided media transmit signals along a physical path (e.g., twisted pair, coaxial, fiber).
Unguided media transmit signals through the air without a physical guide (e.g., microwave, radio).
Guided media offer higher shielding, lower interference, and limited range.
Unguided media enable mobility but are more susceptible to interference and attenuation.

questions.question: Define attenuation and noise in signal transmission. How do they affect the performance of a communication channel?
keyPoints: Attenuation is the reduction in signal strength over distance, measured in decibels (dB).
Noise includes thermal, impulse, intermodulation, and crosstalk; it adds undesired random energy to the signal.
Both increase the bit error rate (BER) and reduce the effective data rate.
Designers use amplifiers, repeaters, and shielding to mitigate attenuation and noise.

questions.question: Discuss the concept of bandwidth in signal transmission. Explain the relationship between bandwidth, data rate, and channel capacity as given by Nyquist and Shannon’s theorems. How does phase modulation affect bandwidth?
keyPoints: Bandwidth is the range of frequencies a channel can transmit, measured in Hz.
Nyquist theorem: maximum data rate = 2B·log₂M (M signal levels) for a noiseless channel.
Shannon theorem: channel capacity C = B·log₂(1 + S/N), linking bandwidth, signal-to-noise ratio, and capacity.
Phase modulation (e.g., BPSK, QPSK) spreads the signal spectrum; higher-order PSK increases data rate but requires greater bandwidth to maintain symbol separation.

questions.question: Compare the different types of guided transmission media (twisted pair, coaxial cable, optical fiber) in terms of attenuation, bandwidth, susceptibility to interference, and typical applications. Also, discuss the advantages of using modulation schemes in unguided media.
keyPoints: Twisted pair: moderate attenuation (~20 dB/km), limited bandwidth (<100 MHz), vulnerable to crosstalk/EMI; used in telephone and Ethernet.
Coaxial cable: lower attenuation, higher bandwidth (up to 1 GHz), better shielding; used in cable TV and high‑speed data.
Optical fiber: very low attenuation (<0.2 dB/km), huge bandwidth (>10 THz), immune to EMI; used in backbone networks and long‑haul links.
Modulation (ASK/FSK/PSK/QAM) in unguided media allows frequency reuse, improves spectral efficiency, and mitigates interference by shifting baseband to RF.
